title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: distinguishable female-male dyad, two members can different residual variances. within-dyad residual covariance block (shared across dyads) : Cov⁡(ϵFiϵMi)=𝚺ϵ=[σϵF2ρϵFϵMσϵFσϵMρϵFϵMσϵFσϵMσϵM2] \operatorname{Cov} \begin{pmatrix} \epsilon_{Fi} \\ \epsilon_{Mi} \end{pmatrix} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\epsilon} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{\epsilon_F}^{2} & \rho_{\epsilon_F\epsilon_M}\sigma_{\epsilon_F}\sigma_{\epsilon_M} \\ \rho_{\epsilon_F\epsilon_M}\sigma_{\epsilon_F}\sigma_{\epsilon_M} & \sigma_{\epsilon_M}^{2} \end{bmatrix} full residual covariance matrix dyads (first three shown) block-diagonal: 𝚺model=[𝚺ϵ𝟎𝟎⋯𝟎𝚺ϵ𝟎⋯𝟎𝟎𝚺ϵ⋯⋮⋮⋮⋱] \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{model}} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\epsilon} & \boldsymbol{0} & \boldsymbol{0} & \cdots \\ \boldsymbol{0} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\epsilon} & \boldsymbol{0} & \cdots \\ \boldsymbol{0} & \boldsymbol{0} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\epsilon} & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{bmatrix} structure estimated unstructured random-effects block us(0 + .dy_is_female_x_male_female + .dy_is_female_x_male_male | coupleID) dispformula = ~ 0.

title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: Conceptually, exchangeable APIM constrains several effects equal: Conceptual cross-sectional APIM exchangeable dyads. two members share one intercept, one actor effect, one partner effect. outcome residuals equal variances, yet still covary within dyads. member labels arbitrary, swapping members 1 2 change model. estimate model univariate MLM framework, can draw conceptual diagram : Individual-level representation exchangeable cross-sectional APIM used long-format multilevel model. members share intercept. member’s predictor shared actor effect, member’s predictor shared partner effect. two residual variances equal residuals may covary.

title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: simple random-intercept structure, equal member variances covariance can specified directly. specification becomes difficult exchangeability constraints must cover several random slopes. single homogeneous structure impose one variance across intercepts slopes, whereas separate structures omit correlations. shared/difference representation works residuals random-effect terms, including random slopes. Following del Rosario West (2025), dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data() generates arbitrary member-difference column, named .dy_member_contrast_*. contrast +1 one member -1 . exchangeable residual structure represented two separate random-effects terms: shared dyad random intercept random coefficient difference column. Additional random slopes can included blocks without changing logic. now fit simple exchangeable APIM use dyadMLM::recover_exchangeable_covariance() function back-transforms structure often interpretable member-level residual covariance matrix.

what: Fitting the exchangeable APIM with glmmTMB
title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: use dataset , distinguish males females. can test distinguishability later comparing model prior model. use set_exchangeable_compositions exchangeability constraints. Another option omit roles. use columns fit model follows: use dyadMLM::recover_exchangeable_covariance() recover interpretable variance-covariance matrix: back-transformation follows directly shared member-difference random effects. uju_j shared effect dyad jj ũj\widetilde{u}_j member-difference effect, two member effects u1j=uj+ũj,u2j=uj−ũj. u_{1j} = u_j + \widetilde{u}_j, \qquad u_{2j} = u_j - \widetilde{u}_j. two fitted blocks independent, Var⁡(u1j)=Var⁡(u2j)=Var⁡(uj)+Var⁡(ũj),Cov⁡(u1j,u2j)=Var⁡(uj)−Var⁡(ũj). \operatorname{Var}(u_{1j}) = \operatorname{Var}(u_{2j}) = \operatorname{Var}(u_j) + \operatorname{Var}(\widetilde{u}_j), \qquad \operatorname{Cov}(u_{1j}, u_{2j}) = \operatorname{Var}(u_j) - \operatorname{Var}(\widetilde{u}_j). output can now mapped follows: Fitted cross-sectional exchangeable APIM example data. common member residual standard deviation residual correlation back-transformed fitted mean difference components.
code: apim_exchangeable_data <- dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data(   dyads_cross,   dyad = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   predictors = provided_support,   keep_compositions = "female-male",   set_exchangeable_compositions = "female-male",   seed = 123 )  print(apim_exchangeable_data, n = 4) #> # dyadMLM data #> # Rows: 240 | Dyads: 120 | Intensive longitudinal: no #> # Structure: dyad = coupleID, member = personID, role = gender #> # #> # Dyad compositions: #> # female_x_male exchangeable (set by user) 120 dyads #> # title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: Distinguishability can evaluated comparing full model two roles may differ restricted exchangeable model. comparison tests imposed equality constraints jointly. , concern fixed intercepts, actor effects, partner effects, residual variances. two parameterizations require different generated columns, models use original observations. dyadMLM::compare_nested_glmmTMB_models() verifies models use equivalent original observations performing likelihood-ratio test: test provides evidence restrictions jointly, show parameter differs. helper also determine whether models mathematically nested; remains modeling requirement. usual chi-squared reference distribution may unreliable tested variance parameter lies boundary.
code: dyadMLM::compare_nested_glmmTMB_models(   apim_exchangeable_model,   apim_distinguishable_model ) #> Likelihood-ratio test for nested models fitted to equivalent data #> Assumes mathematical nesting and an appropriate chi-squared reference distribution. #>  #>                            Df    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance Chisq Chi Df #> apim_exchangeable_model     5 708.23 725.64 -349.12   698.23              #> apim_distinguishable_model  9 615.13 646.46 -298.57   597.13 101.1      4 #>                            Pr(>Chisq)     #> apim_exchangeable_model                   #> apim_distinguishable_model  < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Conclusion (5% level): The likelihood-ratio test provides evidence that `apim_distinguishable_model` fits better than `apim_exchangeable_model` (p < 0.001).

title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: longitudinal APIMs, time-varying predictors decomposed within-person -person components actor partner variables constructed (Bolger Laurenceau 2013; Gistelinck Loeys 2020). default "auto" selects "2l" time predictors supplied. within-person (cwp) component captures occasion-specific deviations member’s observed mean, whereas -person (cbp) component captures member’s observed mean relative sample grand mean. Note observed person means used construct -person (cbp) predictors can unreliable member contributes occasions, can bias -person estimates (Gottfredson 2019).

what: Concurrent ILD Gaussian APIM for distinguishable dyads
title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: decomposition can combined role-specific effects. first retain female-male distinction preparing data: following model allows intercepts, time trends, within- -person actor partner effects differ female male members. Centering diaryday makes role-specific intercepts refer average study day:
code: ild_distinguishable_data <- dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data(   dyads_ild,   dyad = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   time = diaryday,   predictors = provided_support,   model_types = "apim",   keep_compositions = "female-male" ) |>   dplyr::mutate(     # we grand-mean center in this example to help convergence and     # interpretation     diaryday_gmc = diaryday - mean(diaryday)   ) ild_distinguishable_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~     0 +      # Role-specific intercepts     .dy_is_female_x_male_female +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male +      # Role-specific time trends     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:diaryday_gmc +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:diaryday_gmc +      # Role-specific within-person actor effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_cwp_actor +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_cwp_actor +      # Role-specific within-person partner effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_cwp_partner +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_cwp_partner +      # Role-specific between-person actor effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_cbp_actor +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_cbp_actor +      # Role-specific between-person partner effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_cbp_partner +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_cbp_partner +      # Stable dyad-level covariance     us(0 + .dy_is_female_x_male_female + .dy_is_female_x_male_male | coupleID) +      # Same-occasion covariance     us(0 + .dy_is_female_x_male_female + .dy_is_female_x_male_male |          coupleID:diaryday),   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = ild_distinguishable_data ) #> Warning in finalizeTMB(TMBStruc, obj, fit, h, data.tmb.old): Model convergence #> problem; false convergence (8). what: Concurrent ILD Gaussian APIM for exchangeable dyads
title: Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM)
text: longitudinal Gaussian exchangeable APIMs, sum--difference parametrization del Rosario West (2025) can extended dyad-occasion level represent -occasion residual dependence. first prepare within-person (cwp) -person (cbp) actor partner predictors. retain female-female dyads one substantively exchangeable composition: example estimates -day associations support closeness includes diaryday adjust linear time trend. also include actor random slope stable dyad-level blocks can demonstrate random-slope back-transformation . can now recover member-level covariance matrices stable dyad effects -occasion residual dependence. two matched block pairs returned separately: cwp terms estimate actor partner associations occasion-specific deviations member’s usual support. cbp terms estimate actor partner associations involving members’ usual support levels. concurrent associations; represent temporal carryover.

what: Interpretation of concurrent ILD DIM coefficients
title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: mean--deviation interpretation applies longitudinally. Coefficients dyad means describe expected individual outcomes expected couple-average outcomes. Coefficients within-dyad member deviations describe members’ deviations expected difference. cbp dyad-mean estimate (1.55) means , comparing couples whose average usual support differs one point holding stable difference partners constant, expected couple-average closeness 1.55 points higher higher-support couple. Equivalently, member expected report 1.55 points higher closeness. cwp dyad-mean estimate (0.49) means members one point respective usual support levels, member’s expected closeness 0.49 points higher usual levels, holding difference momentary deviations constant. Equivalently, expected couple-average closeness 0.49 points higher occasion. cbp within-dyad member-deviation estimate (0.90) means partners differ one point usual support levels, expected differ 0.90 points closeness, holding couple’s average usual support predictors constant. member terms, suppose one member 0.5 points couple’s average usual support 0.5 points . expected closeness 0.45 points couple’s predicted mean, respectively. cwp within-dyad member-deviation estimate (-0.01) close zero. one partner’s momentary deviation usual support one point higher partner’s deviation, essentially expected closeness difference term, holding occasion-specific dyad mean predictors constant.

what: Equivalence of APIM and DIM in ILD
title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: equivalent APIM uses actor partner effects levels, shown concurrent ILD APIM example. APIM DIM estimate model different coefficients. equivalence holds separately within-person (cwp) -person (cbp) predictor components. within-person component: bcwp,mean=bcwp,actor+bcwp,partner b_{\mathrm{cwp,mean}} = b_{\mathrm{cwp,actor}} + b_{\mathrm{cwp,partner}} bcwp,dev=bcwp,actor−bcwp,partner b_{\mathrm{cwp,dev}} = b_{\mathrm{cwp,actor}} - b_{\mathrm{cwp,partner}} -person component: bcbp,mean=bcbp,actor+bcbp,partner b_{\mathrm{cbp,mean}} = b_{\mathrm{cbp,actor}} + b_{\mathrm{cbp,partner}} bcbp,dev=bcbp,actor−bcbp,partner b_{\mathrm{cbp,dev}} = b_{\mathrm{cbp,actor}} - b_{\mathrm{cbp,partner}} APIM parameterization can therefore used one level DIM parameterization . mixed parameterization changes coefficients, still estimates model.

DSM uses full female-minus-male difference: eF=rm+12rd,eM=rm−12rd. e_{\mathrm{F}} = r_{\mathrm{m}} + \frac{1}{2}r_{\mathrm{d}}, \qquad e_{\mathrm{M}} = r_{\mathrm{m}} - \frac{1}{2}r_{\mathrm{d}}. code: apim_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~     # Role-specific intercepts     0 +     .dy_is_female_x_male_female +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male +      # Role-specific actor effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_actor +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_actor +      # Role-specific partner effects     .dy_is_female_x_male_female:.dy_provided_support_partner +     .dy_is_female_x_male_male:.dy_provided_support_partner +      # Role-specific Gaussian residual covariance structure     us(0 +          .dy_is_female_x_male_female +          .dy_is_female_x_male_male        | coupleID),   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = cross_dsm_data_inverted ) data.frame(   model = c("DSM: female - male", "DSM: male - female", "APIM"),   AIC = round(c(AIC(dsm_model), AIC(dsm_model_inverted), AIC(apim_model)), 3),   BIC = round(c(BIC(dsm_model), BIC(dsm_model_inverted), BIC(apim_model)), 3),   logLik = round(c(     as.numeric(logLik(dsm_model)),     as.numeric(logLik(dsm_model_inverted)),     as.numeric(logLik(apim_model))   ), 3) ) #>                model     AIC    BIC   logLik #> 1 DSM: female - male 615.134 646.46 -298.567 #> 2 DSM: male - female 615.134 646.46 -298.567 #> 3               APIM 615.134 646.46 -298.567

title: Dyadic Score Model (DSM)
text: Let bactor,femaleb_{\mathrm{actor},\mathrm{female}} bactor,maleb_{\mathrm{actor},\mathrm{male}} denote actor effects female male outcomes. corresponding partner effects bpartner,femaleb_{\mathrm{partner},\mathrm{female}} bpartner,maleb_{\mathrm{partner},\mathrm{male}}, APIM intercepts b0,femaleb_{0,\mathrm{female}} b0,maleb_{0,\mathrm{male}}. Fixed APIM coefficients use bb write effect outcome role. numbered paths a10a_{10} a22a_{22} retain published DSM notation. slope transformation can understood two steps. First, outcome role r∈{female,male}r \\{\mathrm{female},\mathrm{male}\}, form actor-plus-partner actor-minus-partner combinations: bsum,r=bactor,r+bpartner,r,bdifference,r=bactor,r−bpartner,r. \begin{aligned} b_{\mathrm{sum},r} &= b_{\mathrm{actor},r} + b_{\mathrm{partner},r}, \\ b_{\mathrm{difference},r} &= b_{\mathrm{actor},r} - b_{\mathrm{partner},r}. \end{aligned} DSM slopes follow combining female- male-outcome effects: a11=bsum,female+bsum,male2,a21=bsum,female−bsum,male,a12=bdifference,female−bdifference,male4,a22=bdifference,female+bdifference,male2. \begin{aligned} a_{11} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}}         + b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}}}{2}, & a_{21} &= b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}}    - b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}}, \\ a_{12} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}}         - b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}}}{4}, & a_{22} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}}         + b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}}}{2}. \end{aligned} APIM predictors retain original scale, whereas DSM predictor level grand-mean centered. Keeping raw APIM predictors original scale preserves reference values; centering difference handled explicitly intercept transformation. μX\mu_X grand mean subtracted DSM predictor level, intercepts transform a10=b0,female+b0,male2+μXa11,a20=b0,female−b0,male+μXa21. a_{10} = \frac{b_{0,\mathrm{female}} + b_{0,\mathrm{male}}}{2} + \mu_X a_{11}, \qquad a_{20} = b_{0,\mathrm{female}} - b_{0,\mathrm{male}} + \mu_X a_{21}. reverse slope transformation, first recover role-specific actor-plus-partner actor-minus-partner combinations: bsum,female=a11+a212,bsum,male=a11−a212,bdifference,female=a22+2a12,bdifference,male=a22−2a12. \begin{aligned} b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}} &= a_{11} + \frac{a_{21}}{2}, & b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}} &= a_{11} - \frac{a_{21}}{2}, \\ b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}} &= a_{22} + 2a_{12}, & b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}} &= a_{22} - 2a_{12}. \end{aligned} , outcome role rr, bactor,r=bsum,r+bdifference,r2,bpartner,r=bsum,r−bdifference,r2. b_{\mathrm{actor},r} = \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},r} + b_{\mathrm{difference},r}}{2}, \qquad b_{\mathrm{partner},r} = \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},r} - b_{\mathrm{difference},r}}{2}. intercepts transform back b0,female=a10+a202−μX(a11+a212), b_{0,\mathrm{female}} = a_{10} + \frac{a_{20}}{2} - \mu_X\left(a_{11} + \frac{a_{21}}{2}\right), b0,male=a10−a202−μX(a11−a212). b_{0,\mathrm{male}} = a_{10} - \frac{a_{20}}{2} - \mu_X\left(a_{11} - \frac{a_{21}}{2}\right). following comparison applies APIM--DSM transformation six fixed effects: APIM--DSM fixed-effect transformation (centering constant = 4.909).

title: Dyadic Score Model (DSM)
text: Let ufemaleu_{\mathrm{female}} umaleu_{\mathrm{male}} APIM random effects female male outcomes. DSM outcome-level outcome-difference residuals random effects expressed different coordinates: (rY,meanrY,diff)=(1/21/21−1)(ufemaleumale). \begin{pmatrix} r_{Y,\mathrm{mean}} \\ r_{Y,\mathrm{diff}} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} u_{\mathrm{female}} \\ u_{\mathrm{male}} \end{pmatrix}. Applying rotation APIM covariance matrix reproduces DSM random intercept variance, intercept-slope covariance, role-slope variance: exchangeable dyads, direction member difference arbitrary. directional intercept, cross-paths, covariance outcome level outcome difference must zero. remaining reduced, label-invariant DSM algebraically Gaussian Dyad-Individual Model (DIM). dyadMLM, use model_types = "dim" exchangeable model reserve model_types = "dsm" distinguishable dyads. Gaussian DIM exchangeability-constrained version full DSM, exchangeability can also tested comparing nested models. equivalent comparison shown Testing distinguishability APIM vignette.
code: apim_vcov <- as.matrix(glmmTMB::VarCorr(apim_model)$cond$coupleID) dsm_vcov <- as.matrix(glmmTMB::VarCorr(dsm_model)$cond$coupleID)  rotation <- rbind(   outcome_level = c(0.5, 0.5),   outcome_difference = c(1, -1) ) apim_to_dsm_vcov <- rotation %*% apim_vcov %*% t(rotation)  data.frame(   parameter = c(     "Var(outcome mean)",     "Cov(outcome mean, outcome diff)",     "Var(outcome diff)"   ),   from_DSM = round(c(     dsm_vcov[1, 1],     dsm_vcov[1, 2],     dsm_vcov[2, 2]   ), 3),   from_APIM_transformation = round(c(     apim_to_dsm_vcov[1, 1],     apim_to_dsm_vcov[1, 2],     apim_to_dsm_vcov[2, 2]   ), 3) ) #>                         parameter from_DSM from_APIM_transformation #> 1               Var(outcome mean)    0.508                    0.508 #> 2 Cov(outcome mean, outcome diff)    0.013                    0.013 #> 3               Var(outcome diff)    0.977                    0.977

DSM retains directional mean--difference parameterization described . detail centering decisions mean--deviation interpretation, refer DIM vignette. dynamic models cautions, see dynamic ILD APIM example. Brief example ILD DSM: example estimates -day associations support closeness includes diaryday allow separate linear trends outcome level female-minus-male outcome difference. cross-sectional path interpretation therefore applies separately within-person -person levels. level, predictor dyad mean directional difference predict outcome level directional outcome difference. Return Actor-Partner Interdependence Model vignette, see Dyad-Individual Model vignette related model specification, return online package overview.

code: install.packages("dyadMLM") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("Pascal-Kueng/dyadMLM")

title: Getting Started
text: dyadMLM helps researchers prepare cross-sectional intensive longitudinal dyadic data (generalized) multilevel models. automatically creates model-ready columns dyadic multilevel model parameterizations Actor-Partner Interdependence Models (APIM), Dyad-Individual Models (DIM), Dyadic Score Models (DSM). DIM currently supports one exchangeable dyad composition, whereas DSM supports one distinguishable dyad composition. vignette focuses automatic data preparation multilevel models (MLMs). comparison MLM structural equation modeling (SEM) approaches dyadic data, see Ledermann Kenny (2017). available model-specific vignettes include Actor-Partner Interdependence Model, Dyad-Individual Model, Dyadic Score Model. online package overview provides current online versions vignettes complete function reference.

title: Getting Started
text: basic data structure needed dyadMLM long data frame dyads stacked top members dyad appear separate rows. raw data currently wide format (time dyads ), reshape long structure first. See tidyr pivoting vignette pivot_longer() reference. Roughly, expected structure dyadMLM : cross-sectional data: one row per dyad x member intensive longitudinal data: one row per dyad x time x member Measured variables may contain missing values. structural dyad, member, optional time variables must contain missing values. intensive longitudinal data, missing measurement occasions can represented absent rows, long time variable preserves observed measurement occasions. example: example, row person 1 time 2 absent. time variable preserves observed measurement occasions skips time 1 time 3 person. dyadMLM accepts structure without requiring placeholder row missing occasion.

title: Getting Started
text: dyads_cross contains three dyad compositions. dyad two rows: one member. closeness provided-support scores member-level averages across 14 days dyads_ild. example data contain multiple compositions, keep_compositions selects composition modeled . can omitted supplied data already contain intended composition. validate prepare data function dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data(). function retained recognized 120 female-male dyads created APIM-relevant variables (Kenny Cook 1999). generated .dy_* columns can used directly model formulas. fitted APIM examples using columns, see Actor-Partner Interdependence Model vignette.

title: Getting Started
text: exchangeable dyads, can request DIM predictor columns. DIM preparation requires exactly one exchangeable composition, retain keep_compositions. Generating DSM columns distinguishable dyads additionally requires explicit role order. role order defines direction DSM predictor differences DSM role contrast (Iida et al. 2018).

title: Getting Started
text: dyads_ild intensive longitudinal dyadic dataset. dyad repeated observations diaryday, one row per person-day. prepare intensive longitudinal data, pass time variable dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data(). default, numeric predictors longitudinal APIM preparation decomposed within-person -person components (Bolger Laurenceau 2013). temporal predictor decomposition controlled temporal_decomposition. default "auto" setting selects "2l" longitudinal setup retains raw actor partner columns alongside components. Note observed person means used construct -person (cbp) predictors can unreliable member contributes occasions, can bias -person estimates (Gottfredson 2019).

title: Getting Started
text: Lagged versions variables, including outcome also passed predictors, can obtained lag1_predictors argument. dyadMLM::prepare_dyad_data() returns lag-1 raw within-person-centered actor partner columns alongside contemporaneous versions. Lagging respects dyad member structure, matches observations exactly time - 1, bridge missing occasions. simpler dynamic model, example retains exchangeable female-female composition: Note: Whether use raw within-person-centered lagged outcome depends research question data. Including lagged outcome dynamic models can introduce bias, especially shorter time series (Hamaker Grasman 2015; Nickell 1981; Gistelinck et al. 2021). See dynamic ILD APIM example detailed discussion guidance.

title: Tools for Dyadic Multilevel Models
text: dyadMLM provides tools dyadic multilevel modeling linear generalized linear mixed-effects models. validates prepares cross-sectional intensive longitudinal dyadic data. supports distinguishable exchangeable dyads can also prepare datasets containing multiple dyad compositions. creates composition-aware, model-ready columns dyadic multilevel model parameterizations Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM), Dyad-Individual Model (DIM), Dyadic Score Model (DSM). Selected post-estimation tools compare compatible fitted models back-transform exchangeable random-effect covariance structures member-level quantities. Start vignettes, scroll quick-start. -depth tutorial covering data preparation, model fitting, diagnostics, assumption checks, see Distinguishable Exchangeable Dyads: Bayesian Multilevel Modelling. uses dyadMLM cross-sectional intensive longitudinal APIM DIM workflows, models fitted primarily using brms (source, DOI).

title: Tools for Dyadic Multilevel Models
text: Prepare distinguishable dyads cross-sectional APIM: prepared data contains composition indicators APIM actor/partner predictor columns used model formulas . One simple distinguishable APIM formula :
code: library(dyadMLM)  prepared_data <- prepare_dyad_data(   dyads_cross,   dyad = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   predictors = provided_support,   model_types = "apim",   # dyads_cross contains three compositions; retain `female-male` here.   keep_compositions = "female-male" )  print(prepared_data, n = 4) #> # dyadMLM data #> # Rows: 240 | Dyads: 120 | Intensive longitudinal: no #> # Structure: dyad = coupleID, member = personID, role = gender #> # #> # Dyad compositions: #> # female_x_male distinguishable 120 dyads #> # #> # Added columns: #> #   .dy_composition       inferred dyad composition #> #   .dy_composition_role  composition-specific member role #> #   .dy_is_{comp-role}    composition-role indicator columns #> #   .dy_{pred}_actor      APIM actor predictor: actor's original predictor #> #                         values #> #   .dy_{pred}_partner    APIM partner predictor: partner's original predictor #> #                         values #> # #> # A tibble: 240 × 12 #>   personID coupleID gender dyad_composition closeness provided_support #>      <int>    <int> <fct>  <fct>                <dbl>            <dbl> #> 1        1        1 female female_x_male         4.77             4.49 #> 2        2        1 male   female_x_male         4.46             4.76 #> 3        3        2 female female_x_male         6.44             4.09 #> 4        4        2 male   female_x_male         5.99             6.20 #> # ℹ 236 more rows #> # ℹ 6 more variables: .dy_composition <fct>, .dy_composition_role <fct>, #> #   .dy_is_female_x_male_female <dbl>, .dy_is_female_x_male_male <dbl>, #> #   .dy_provided_support_actor <dbl>, .dy_provided_support_partner <dbl> simple_apim <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~      # Gender-specific intercepts     0 + .dy_is_female_x_male_female + .dy_is_female_x_male_male +      # Gender-specific actor effects     .dy_provided_support_actor:.dy_is_female_x_male_female +     .dy_provided_support_actor:.dy_is_female_x_male_male +      # Gender-specific partner effects     .dy_provided_support_partner:.dy_is_female_x_male_female +     .dy_provided_support_partner:.dy_is_female_x_male_male +      # Dyad-level random effects represent the two members'     # residual covariance structure     us(0 + .dy_is_female_x_male_female + .dy_is_female_x_male_male | coupleID),    # With the residual covariance represented by the dyad-level   # random effects above, the Gaussian residual dispersion is fixed near zero.   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = prepared_data )

title: Compare nested glmmTMB models fitted to equivalent data — compare_nested_glmmTMB_models
code: if (requireNamespace("glmmTMB", quietly = TRUE)) {   restricted_data <- prepare_dyad_data(     dyads_cross,     dyad = coupleID,     member = personID,     role = gender,     # dyads_cross contains three compositions; retain `female-male` here.     keep_compositions = "female-male"   )   full_data <- restricted_data    restricted_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(     closeness ~ 1 + us(1 | coupleID),     data = restricted_data   )   full_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(     closeness ~ gender + us(1 | coupleID),     data = full_data   )    compare_nested_glmmTMB_models(restricted_model, full_model) } #> Likelihood-ratio test for nested models fitted to equivalent data #> Assumes mathematical nesting and an appropriate chi-squared reference distribution. #>  #>                  Df    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Chi Df Pr(>Chisq)     #> restricted_model  3 882.71 893.15 -438.36   876.71                              #> full_model        4 813.42 827.35 -402.71   805.42 71.289      1  < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Conclusion (5% level): The likelihood-ratio test provides evidence that `full_model` fits better than `restricted_model` (p < 0.001).

text: data data frame tibble. Data must long format. cross-sectional dyadic data, observed member dyad one row. intensive longitudinal dyadic data, observed member dyad one row per observed time point. dyad Column identifying dyad. member Column identifying person member within dyad. role Optional column identifying stable member role, gender. Non-missing values must consistent within dyad x member must contain _x_. repeated-measures data, observed role propagated missing rows member within dyad. missing_role controls dyads member non-missing role row. role supplied, dyads treated type exchangeable dyads. time Optional column identifying time measurement order repeated measures. predictors Optional variables use temporal predictor decomposition model-ready predictor construction. lag1_predictors Optional subset predictors lag-1 model-ready columns created. Requires time finite, integer-valued numeric measurement index. Lagging respects dyad member structure, matches observations exactly time - 1, bridge missing occasions. raw within-person predictors lagged. Stable -person versions . model_types Model-ready column families construct. Can contain one "apim", "dim", "dsm". "apim" creates actor partner predictors. "dim" creates dyad-mean within-dyad member-deviation predictors. "dsm" creates dyadic-score model predictor columns. "none" skips model-specific predictor construction validation, composition inference, optional temporal predictor decomposition, must used alone. "dim" "dsm" must requested separate calls. dsm_role_order model_types = "dsm", character vector giving two distinguishable roles order used directional differences. example, c("female", "male") defines predictor differences female minus male assigns DSM role contrast +0.5 female partners -0.5 male partners. Required DSM columns requested must NULL otherwise. temporal_decomposition Temporal decomposition strategy predictors. "none" leaves predictors undecomposed model-specific columns constructed. "2l" indicates two-level temporal predictor decomposition within-person -person components. "auto" resolves "2l" time predictors supplied, "none" otherwise. "2l" retains raw model-ready predictors addition within-person -person components. longitudinal DIM DSM construction, raw within-person dyadic scores computed within dyad occasion, -person scores computed within dyads. Raw DIM DSM dyad means grand-mean centered. include raw, within-person, -person versions contemporaneous predictor one model linearly dependent. set_exchangeable_compositions Optionally specify dyad compositions treat exchangeable, roles otherwise imply distinguishability. Requires role. Compositions already exchangeable listed. composition must supplied one string, using _x_, -, _, whitespace ( ) two role labels, example "female_x_male", "female-male", "female_male", "female male", arbitrary order. set multiple compositions, use character vector strings. keep_compositions Optional observed dyad compositions keep exchangeability overrides pooling. Requires role. Composition references use format set_exchangeable_compositions. NULL keeps observed compositions. pool_compositions Optionally pool exchangeable dyad compositions shared final composition label. Must named list name final composition label value character vector composition references, example list(same_sex_couples = c("female-female", "male-male")). exchangeable compositions can pooled. pool must contain least two distinct observed compositions composition references resolved. incomplete_dyads handle dyads fewer two unique members across rows data. "error" stops error "drop" removes entire dyad. dyad two unique members invalid always causes error, regardless setting. missing_role handle dyads least one member non-missing role value row. consistent non-missing role observed member propagated member's rows policy applied. "error" stops error "drop" removes entire dyad. Conflicting non-missing roles always cause error. Ignored role column supplied. seed Optional seed random .dy_member_contrast_* sign assignment exchangeable dyads. NULL, current R session's RNG state used.

title: dyadMLM 0.0.1
text: Initial release. Cleaned pre-release public API direct migration mappings: group dyad, lag_predictors lag1_predictors, model_type model_types, temporal_predictor_decomposition temporal_decomposition, "time_2l" "2l", include_compositions keep_compositions, compare_dyad_models() compare_nested_glmmTMB_models(), exchangeable_rescov() recover_exchangeable_covariance(), pairs block_pairings, pairing fields shared difference shared_block difference_block, print argument representation. Renamed generated exchangeable-member contrasts .dy_diff_{composition}_arbitrary .dy_member_contrast_{composition}_arbitrary. Covariance recovery continues recognize legacy contrast names previously fitted models. Renamed package interdep dyadMLM; package-generated columns now use .dy_ prefix instead .i_. Added validation preparation cross-sectional intensive longitudinal dyadic data distinguishable, exchangeable, mixed dyad compositions. Added composition filtering, exchangeability overrides, pooling. Exchangeable-dyad difference columns now use _arbitrary suffix. Added temporal predictor decomposition model-ready columns APIM, DIM, DSM parameterizations. Added compare_nested_glmmTMB_models() compatible nested glmmTMB models recover_exchangeable_covariance() back-transforming shared/difference random-effect covariance structures. Added example datasets, getting-started model-specific vignettes, concise print method prepared data.
